text: with line after line of binary sequences that ultimately “created” a three-dimensional facsimile of the “real” world of “first life.” Many argue that the “real” world is a mind-created digital illusion. This proposal therefore suggests that Second Life is simply an illusion within an illusion. Indeed, I am reminded of those nested Russian dolls, each of which contains another doll. As we shall discover later, American quantum physicist David Bohm anticipated exactly such a scenario when he suggested that, at its deepest level, the universe is enfolded within itself, in something he termed the holomovement . In the 1970s businessman Robert Monroe had a series of out-of-body experiences that led him to question the model of reality that he had believed in up until that time. He was keen to understand exactly what was taking place when he found himself in “alternate realities.” He attracted a strong team of scientists and researchers to his work and set up the Monroe Institute to allow the team to work on ways of facilitating such states at will. One young physicist who joined him was Tom Campbell. Campbell helped design experiments and develop new technologies that would assist in this aim. Campbell was fascinated by his own experience at the Monroe Institute and has now developed his own model of reality, which involves an interaction between what he terms physical matter reality (PMR) and non-physical matter reality (NPMR). In his trilogy of books, collectively entitled My Big Toe , Campbell suggests that both PMR and NPMR are actually elements of a huge computer program, which “creates” the perceived world in the same way that the humble PC renders a first-person virtual-reality computer game. 3 When I discussed this with Campbell recently, I was immediately reminded of Second Life. In his lectures Campbell discusses how the program (run on something he half-jokingly refers to as the TBC—“The Big Computer”) works on similar principles, in that, to save processing time, “reality” as perceived by any “observer” is rendered only in the direction of the attention of that observer. So for example, within your present version of PMR the only visual information rendered is what you can see with your eyes. Your peripheral vision, which will look blurred as you look forward, contains less rendered information than what is in front of you. What is behind you is literally nothing. As you turn your head, it “pops” into existence, only to disappear again when you look away. If Campbell is correct, then we are existing within nested illusory realities, where the dream state is one of many. In this way, dreams, lucid or otherwise, are as “real” as consensual reality, which, in turn, is an externally generated illusion. One of the most fascinating books on lucid dreaming that I have read is Thomas Yuschak’s Advanced Lucid Dreaming: The Power of Supplements . Yuschak is a very experienced lucid dreamer and, like many others, he is intrigued by the similarities between the lucid-dream experience and ecsomatic states such as the NDE and the OBE. According to Yuschak there are two types of lucid dreams. He explains that among lucid-dream enthusiasts these are called “DILDs” (dream-induced lucid dreams) and “WILDs” (wake-induced lucid dreams). A DILD is the classic lucid dream in that the person becomes aware while in a dream state. A WILD involves an immediate change from a “waking state” to a lucid-dream state, with no loss of consciousness. This transition is usually accompanied by, as Yuschak describes it, “some intense sensations such as a feeling of floating, strong vibrations, and/or rapid accelerations.” 4 Yuschak believes that when he experiences a WILD and leaves his body (in a state identical to that of an OBE), the room he finds himself in is a facsimile of the real room and that it is created and modeled by his mind. This illusion facilitates the unbroken transition from the fully awake state to that of the lucid-dream state as encountered in a WILD. He points out that this happens in 40 percent of his WILDs. He describes a second transitional state during a WILD, in which his body seems to be accelerated toward the location where the WILD will take place. Another important point is that Yuschak regularly describes seeing his body in the WILD and how everything is not at all “dreamlike.” In articles suggesting that lucid dreams and OBEs are different, these are two of the elements that are cited as classic differentials. A third commonly cited differential is that in a lucid dream there is no transition state—the dreamer just finds themselves somewhere—but clearly this is exactly what Yuschak does not experience. He has a very clear transition state, identical in every way to an OBE, including the lifting out of the body and seeing it on the bed. Yuschak also makes the point that in a WILD there is a continuity of awareness and that the lucid dreamers are fully aware of their personality and memories. They know exactly who they are and have access to their memory history. He states: “Lucid dreamers consider this to be a transition into a dream whereas Astral Travellers believe it is real.” 5 This is a very important point. In a fascinating description of this transition state, Yuschak describes how sometimes he feels as if he is “rolling over” and then finds himself floating at a point near the ceiling. Does this not remind you of Monroe’s “rolling-over technique”? You may recall that when Monroe encountered his first fully ecsomatic state he found himself in a similar position to that described by Yuschak—bouncing off the ceiling of his bedroom. Indeed, many of Yuschak’s descriptions remind me of Monroe’s encounters. In Journeys Out of the Body , Monroe describes a vivid “semi-dream” in which he found himself feeling the rug on his bedroom floor and then describing his amazement as his fingers went through the floor and into the space between his bedroom floor and the ceiling of the room
